---
title: "Gen3: Public BTS Replication"
subtitle: "Nationwide Public Data | 19,153,634 flights | Reproducible Academic Research"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-location: right
    toc-depth: 2
    number-sections: false
    page-layout: full
    smooth-scroll: true
---

```{=html}
<nav style="margin-bottom:1.5rem;">
  <small><a href="index.html">Home</a> › <a href="research-overview-en.html">Research Overview</a> › Gen3</small>
  <br>
  <a href="research-overview-en.html" style="display:inline-block;margin-top:0.5rem;padding:0.3rem 0.8rem;border:1px solid #ccc;border-radius:4px;text-decoration:none;font-size:0.9rem;">← Back to Research Overview</a>
</nav>
```

---

::: {.panel-tabset}

## Overview {#overview}

### Why Gen3?

Gen2 uses American Airlines' proprietary Snowflake database, which cannot be publicly reproduced — a major limitation for academic papers. Gen3's goal is:

> **Achieve reproducible and auditable results on fully public data using the same methodological framework.**

### Data Sources

| Item | Details |
|------|---------|
| Primary Data | Bureau of Transportation Statistics (BTS) US DOT |
| Weather Data | Iowa Environmental Mesonet (IEM) METAR + TAF |
| Time Range | 2021-01-01 to 2023-12-31 (36 months) |
| Total Flights | **19,153,634** |
| Scope | All US airlines, all airports |
| Label | delayed_15 (arrival delay ≥ 15 minutes) |
| Temporal Split | Train ≤ 2022-12 / Val 2023-01–06 / Test 2023-07–12 |

### Gen3 vs Gen2

| Dimension | Gen2 | Gen3 |
|-----------|------|------|
| Data Source | AA Snowflake (proprietary) | BTS (public) |
| Scope | Single airline DFW Hub | All US flights |
| Flights | 804,728 | 19,153,634 (x23.8) |
| Reproducibility | ❌ | ✅ |
| Leakage Control | Strict temporal split | **12-Rule Leakage Checklist** |

### 12-Rule Leakage Control

Gen3 designed explicit availability audit rules for every feature, ensuring all features are obtainable at T-2h to T-6h before departure:

::: {.callout-tip}
**34/34 features passed the 12-Rule Leakage Checklist ✅**

Schedule features: Known hours/days before departure, all safe
Airport static: Long-term stable data, safe
Calendar features: Astronomical facts, safe
METAR weather: merge_asof(backward) ensures only observations with timestamp ≤ cutoff time
TAF forecasts: Uses forecast time ≤ T-2h with validity period covering arrival time
Tail chain features: Uses actual arrival time to determine if previous flight is known (same V9c fix)
:::

### Gen3 Version Architecture

| Version | Description | Best AUC |
|---------|-------------|---------|
| **V4 (Clean Baseline)** | 34 features, default hyperparameters, full SHAP analysis | **0.7011** |
| **V2 (Full Pipeline)** | 41 features, Optuna HPO, 3-model comparison | **0.7681** |
| **V5 (Prescriptive)** | Reverse-engineering V4 CatBoost for scheduling recommendations | — |

---

## V4 Clean Baseline {#v4-baseline}

### Design

V4 is Gen3's clean baseline experiment: using only the 34 features that passed the 12-rule leakage checklist, with default hyperparameters (no HPO), three-model comparison.

**34 Features by Group:**

| Feature Group | Count |
|--------------|-------|
| Schedule | — |
| Airport Static | — |
| Calendar | — |
| Origin METAR (origin weather) | — |
| Dest METAR (destination weather) | — |
| TAF Forecasts | — |
| **Total** | **34** |

Note: V4 excludes tail chain and congestion features (the main difference between V4 Clean and V2 Full Pipeline).

### Results

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
**V4 Best Model: CatBoost, AUC = 0.7011**
:::

| Model | AUC (Full features) |
|-------|---------------------|
| Random Forest | — |
| XGBoost | — |
| **CatBoost** | **0.7011** |

### V4 vs V2 Gap Analysis

::: {.callout-tip}
**V4 (Clean Baseline): AUC = 0.7011**
**V2 (Full Pipeline): AUC = 0.7681**
**Gap: −0.0670**

This gap quantifies the combined contribution of four factors:
1. Tail Chain features (6)
2. Congestion features (3)
3. Optuna HPO
4. SHAP feature selection
:::

V4's SHAP analysis (CatBoost, 50K test samples) provides a feature importance baseline under "no tail chain and no congestion" conditions.

---

## V2 Full Pipeline {#v2-pipeline}

### Design

V2 adds to V4's 34 clean features:
- Tail chain features (6)
- Congestion features (3)
- SHAP backward elimination feature selection (41 → 41, result: no features removed, all retained)
- Optuna TPE Bayesian HPO (100 trials)

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
**Optuna Best Results**

Subsample Val AUC: 0.7559 (2M training sample subset)
Full-train Val AUC: **0.7642** (full 12.4M training set validation)
Best iteration count: 4995
HPO time: 3238 seconds (54.0 minutes)
:::

### Results

::: {.callout-tip}
**V2 Best: XGBoost (Full 41 features), AUC = 0.7681**
:::

| Model | Full AUC | Selected AUC | ΔAUC |
|-------|----------|--------------|------|
| Random Forest | 0.7497 | 0.7497 | +0.0000 |
| **XGBoost (Optuna)** | **0.7681** | **0.7681** | **+0.0000** |
| CatBoost | 0.7627 | 0.7627 | +0.0000 |

SHAP feature selection removed 0 features in Gen3 V2 (41→41 all retained), confirming the conservativeness of the backward elimination stopping criterion (ΔAUC > 0.001) on nationwide large-scale datasets.

---

## V5 Prescriptive {#v5-prescriptive}

### Background & Motivation

> **Advisor's directive: "Don't focus on proving how well you can train models... find what's inside the black box... how should schedules be arranged to avoid flight delays? Methodology is what matters most."**

V5 does not modify the model or pursue AUC — instead, it **reverse-engineers the V4 CatBoost (AUC = 0.7011)** to extract actionable scheduling recommendations.

| Item | Details |
|------|---------|
| Model | V4 CatBoost (AUC=0.7011, 34 features, iterations=1000) |
| Data | gen3_v4_feat.parquet (19.15M rows × 36 cols) |
| Analyses | 11 analyses (Part A: 6 + Part B: 5) |
| Output | 40 figures + 8 JSONs + 2 reports (EN/ZH) |

---

### Part A: SHAP Reverse Engineering

#### 1. SHAP Dependence Deep Dive

SHAP dependence plots for the top-10 features with automatic interaction coloring, revealing non-linear relationships:

- **dep_hour**: Risk increases sharply in afternoon hours
- **distance**: Short-haul flights show higher delay risk
- **schd_block_min**: More buffer time reduces delay probability
- **carrier_encoded**: Significant variation across airlines
- **is_holiday_period**: Clear binary increase in delay risk

#### 2. SHAP Interaction Analysis

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
CatBoost does not support `shap_interaction_values` → trained XGBoost surrogate (AUC = 0.7009), 15K subsample for interaction matrix computation.
:::

6 key interaction effects:

| Interaction Pair | Finding |
|-----------------|---------|
| dep_hour × distance | Afternoon short-haul is more dangerous |
| dep_hour × carrier | Airline peak management capability varies |
| dep_hour × wx_severity | Weather × peak compound effect |
| distance × schd_block_min | Long-haul needs more buffer |
| is_holiday_period × dep_hour | Which holiday time slots are most vulnerable |
| wx_temp_f × dep_month | Seasonal weather effects |

#### 3. PDP + ICE + ALE Cross-Validation

Three independent methods (PDP, ICE, ALE) cross-validate SHAP findings. ALE corrects for correlated feature bias (e.g., distance and block time correlation). Includes 2D PDP contour plots: dep_hour × distance, dep_hour × schd_block_min.

#### 4. Counterfactual What-If Analysis

::: {.callout-tip}
**Holding weather constant, varying only scheduling variables, testing 4 interventions on 1,000 delayed flights:**

| Intervention | Baseline Delay | Post-Intervention | Reduction |
|-------------|---------------|-------------------|-----------|
| **A. Morning shift (6 AM)** | 63.1% | 10.1% | **−53.0pp** |
| B. Buffer increase (+45 min) | 63.1% | 46.0% | −17.1pp |
| C. Non-holiday (472 holiday flights) | 76.7% | 64.4% | −12.3pp |
| D. Good weather + morning | 52.8% | 19.2% | −33.6pp |
| D. Bad weather + morning | 73.4% | 33.4% | **−40.0pp** |

**Morning shift is the most effective single intervention: delay rate drops from 63.1% to 10.1%.**
:::

#### 5. Risk Profiling

All test flights (3.46M) classified into 5 risk quintiles by predicted delay probability:

| Metric | Q1 (Safest) | Q5 (Riskiest) |
|--------|-------------|---------------|
| Actual Delay Rate | 6.8% | 37.1% |
| Avg Departure Time | 9:00 AM | 4:40 PM |
| Avg Distance | 691 mi | 874 mi |
| Avg Block Time | 130 min | 149 min |
| Holiday % | 22.9% | 59.4% |
| Top Carriers | DL, WN, UA | WN, AA, UA |

---

### Part B: Mathematical Feature Extraction

#### 6. Surrogate Decision Tree

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
**Shallow decision tree (depth=5) approximating CatBoost predictions**

Fidelity = **0.7985** | 32 leaf nodes

Surrogate feature importance: dep_hour (54.2%) > carrier (16.8%) > holiday (15.8%) > taf_dest_has_precip (5.0%) > wx_precip_1hr_in (4.1%)
:::

The surrogate tree extracted human-readable IF-THEN rules (98 lines), core logic:

- **dep_hour ≤ 12.5** AND **dep_hour ≤ 8.5** AND no destination precipitation → **No delay**
- **dep_hour > 12.5** AND **carrier = specific airline** AND holiday → **Delay**

#### 7. Piecewise Linear Breakpoints

3-segment piecewise linear regression on SHAP curves, quantifying exact risk inflection points:

::: {.callout-tip}
| Feature | Breakpoint 1 | Breakpoint 2 | R² |
|---------|-------------|-------------|-----|
| **dep_hour** | **5.5h** | **15.5h** | 0.943 |
| distance | 786 mi | 2,639 mi | 0.986 |
| schd_block_min | 101 min | 342 min | 0.995 |
| wx_temp_f | 43.6°F | 69.2°F | 0.974 |
| wx_severity | 0.35 | 0.37 | 0.985 |

**dep_hour breakpoints**: Risk drops rapidly before 5:30 AM, rises sharply after 3:30 PM.
:::

#### 8. Symbolic Regression

gplearn genetic programming (population=1000, generations=30) discovered a closed-form formula:

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
**Formula: log(log(log(Departure Hour)))**

R² = 0.3795 | Length = 4 | Depth = 3

Interpretation: Departure hour's impact on delays follows a highly non-linear triple logarithmic compression — risk changes dramatically from dawn to morning, then plateaus from afternoon to evening.
:::

#### 9. SHAP Clustering — Delay Archetypes

K-Means (elbow method → K=4) clustering on SHAP value vectors revealed 4 delay archetypes:

| Cluster | Share | Delay Rate | Dominant Driver | Interpretation |
|---------|-------|-----------|----------------|----------------|
| C0 | 38.8% | 19.6% | distance (+) | Long-haul delay type |
| C1 | 13.4% | 24.9% | dest_precip | Weather precipitation type |
| C2 | 46.9% | 14.5% | distance (−) | Short-haul normal type |
| **C3** | **0.9%** | **51.4%** | **wx_severity** | **Extreme weather type** |

::: {.callout-warning}
**C3 (Extreme Weather)** accounts for only 0.9% of flights but has a 51.4% delay rate — this is the only scenario where weather truly dominates delays, consistent with the overall "weather near-zero" finding: weather is only the primary driver in extreme cases.
:::

---

### Prescriptive Recommendations Summary

::: {.callout-tip}
**Optimal departure windows**: 5:00–8:00 AM (safest) | 18:00–21:00 PM (riskiest)

**Buffer recommendations**: +15 min → 57.5% delay | +30 min → 50.9% | +45 min → 46.0%

**Carrier benchmarks**: Best: 9E | Worst: G4 | AA = SHAP +0.054

**Most effective interventions**: Morning shift (−53.0pp) > Bad weather + morning (−40.0pp) > Buffer +45min (−17.1pp) > Non-holiday (−12.3pp)
:::

---

## SHAP Source-Group Analysis {#shap}

### Method

Grouped 41 features by data source, computed SHAP contribution sums for XGBoost (AUC=0.7681) on 50K test samples.

### Gen3 Results

::: {.callout-tip}
**Gen3 SHAP Source-Group Analysis Results (XGBoost, 19.15M flights)**

| Source Group | SHAP% |
|-------------|-------|
| Tail Chain | **35.5%** |
| Congestion | 14.4% |
| Schedule | 18.6% |
| Weather (METAR + TAF) | Low |

**dep_hour is the single most important feature (Schedule group #1 feature).**
:::

### Gen2 vs Gen3 SHAP Comparison

| Source | Gen2 (V9d, 804K AA DFW) | Gen3 V2 (19.15M nationwide) |
|--------|--------------------------|---------------------------|
| Tail Chain | 22.7% | **35.5%** |
| Schedule | 22.2% | 18.6% |
| Aircraft/Infrastructure | 20.5% | — |
| Congestion | (included in Aircraft/Infra) | 14.4% |
| DFW METAR | 14.4% | Low |
| TAF Forecast | 12.4% | Low |
| Dest METAR | 7.7% | Low |

**Cross-dataset robustness**: Tail chain features are the most important feature group across two completely different datasets (proprietary AA vs public BTS, single Hub vs nationwide), supporting the generalizability of our conclusions.

---

## Schedule Structural Dominance {#schedule}

### Core Finding

::: {.callout-tip}
**"You can tell if a flight will be delayed just by looking at the schedule"**

Under strict leakage control, schedule-derived features (dep_hour, dep_month, is_holiday_period, etc.) systematically dominate predictions. More than half of the predictable portion of delays is determined the moment the schedule is set.
:::

### What This Means

**Delays are not random accidents — they are structural problems.** Certain time slots (morning vs evening), certain months (around holidays), and certain routes have inherently higher delay risk built into the schedule.

Gen2 ablation data supports this conclusion:
- Schedule-only baseline: AUC = 0.6226
- Full features (43): AUC = 0.7279
- Schedule features explain **~56%** of predictable variance (0.6226 / 0.7279)

### Gap in the Literature

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
**No paper has ever quantified "what proportion is schedule-dominated and why" using SHAP under strict leakage control.**

FDPP-ML (Journal of Big Data, 2023) noted that delays can be predicted using only schedule features, but did not explain why or quantify the proportion.

We rigorously validate this at 19.15M flight scale — this is not a geographic anomaly but a systematic structural finding.
:::

### Implications for the Aviation Industry

If delays are primarily determined by schedule structure, then:
- Flight scheduling optimization is more effective than real-time intervention
- Overcrowded scheduling during high-delay-probability periods is the fundamental problem
- The core value of "advance prediction" is enabling passengers and operators to prepare early, not "learning about delays only when they happen"

---

## Weather Near-Zero Contribution {#weather}

### Core Finding

::: {.callout-tip}
**Under strict pre-departure leakage control, weather features' SHAP contribution in Gen3 is near zero (relative to tail chain and schedule).**

This contradicts most literature conclusions — but we believe this is a methodological difference, not a real-world difference.
:::

### Why Weather is Unimportant in Our Model

**Physical reasons**:
- METAR weather observations 2–6 hours before departure represent "current" weather, not weather at arrival time
- Long-haul flights (e.g., DFW → JFK, 3–4 hour flight time) have limited destination weather prediction accuracy at departure time
- TAF forecast temporal resolution (typical forecast intervals of 1–6 hours) means forecasts still differ from actual arrival weather

**Methodological reasons (most critical)**:

::: {.callout-warning}
**Why weather appears "important" in existing literature**

Many papers compute weather features using post-departure or post-arrival weather observations (actual departure time, METAR at arrival), constituting information leakage. Under strict T-2h cutoff conditions, these "future weather" values are unavailable, and weather's apparent importance drops dramatically.
:::

### Comparison with Other Literature

| Paper | Weather Importance | Attribution |
|-------|-------------------|-------------|
| Hatipoglu & Tosun (2024, MDPI) | Low | Stable Turkish climate |
| Alfarhood et al. (2024, Wiley) | Low | Saudi Arabian desert climate |
| **This study** | **Low** | **Methodology (leakage control), not geography** |

::: {.callout-tip}
**Our contribution**: The US has complex and variable weather. Weather being "still unimportant" can only be explained by methodology — under strict pre-departure leakage control, weather's apparent importance may be primarily an artifact of measurement bias.

**No paper has ever presented this finding in a strict leakage-controlled experiment at US continental scale.**
:::

### Gen2 Ablation Evidence

| Operation | AUC | Δ |
|-----------|-----|---|
| Full model (with weather) | 0.7279 | — |
| Remove DFW METAR | 0.7255 | −0.0024 (marginal) |
| **Remove Dest METAR** | **0.7380** | **+0.0101 (removal improves performance!)** |
| **Remove TAF Forecast** | **0.7285** | **+0.0006 (virtually no difference)** |

> After tail chain features are included, weather features' marginal contribution to the model is near zero or even negative — tail chain features have already absorbed weather's predictive signal.

:::

---

```{=html}
<script>
window.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', function() {
  var hash = window.location.hash.replace('#', '');
  if (!hash) return;
  var tabs = document.querySelectorAll('[data-bs-toggle="tab"], [data-bs-toggle="pill"]');
  for (var i = 0; i < tabs.length; i++) {
    var target = tabs[i].getAttribute('data-bs-target') || tabs[i].getAttribute('href') || '';
    if (target.indexOf(hash) !== -1) {
      tabs[i].click();
      break;
    }
  }
});
</script>
```
